text: Fistula in Anno, Lipocytes, Regenerative, Therapy. A video technique.
We used this novel approach for the treatment of complex perianal fistula. After failure of all previous treatments.
All patients were previously treated with leucetan insertion.
The surgeon makes a five-millimeter subumbilical incision using a #15 scalpel blade.
The surgeon infiltrates the subcutaneous fat in a fan-shaped manner using a solution of 250 milliliters of saline, 50 milliliters of 2% lidocaine, and 1 milliliter of adrenaline.
The surgeon uses a standard 50 milliliter syringe connected to a 20-gauge cannula, inserted through the subumbilical incision.

Ten minutes after infiltration, the surgeon performs liposuction using a fifty milliliter syringe connected to a circulation procurement system, applying negative suction pressure to remove subcutaneous fat.
The surgeon closes the peri-umbilical incision at the end of the liposuction procedure.
The lipoaspirate is transferred into fifty milliliter syringes and positioned horizontally, facilitating the visual separation of excess fluids from the desired fat tissue.
The surgeon processes mesenchymal cells using an allopospiration technique, followed by the deployment of an adipose tissue device.

The surgeon uses a fistuloscope to visualize the anatomy of the fistula tract, identifying any residual abscesses, side tracts, and the internal opening. The fistuloscope is also used to curette and clean the fistula tract.

Following infiltration with adrenalized saline, the surgeon creates an advancement flap to cover the internal opening of the fistula. The flap, composed of mucosa, submucosa, and muscular fibers, is secured at the distal margin with absorbable sutures.
The surgeon infiltrates the lipoaspirate around the external opening and along the entire fistulous tract, ensuring thorough coverage of the affected area.
